title_en: Elite Young Runners Are Becoming Freakishly Fast. Welcome to ‘Trackflation’
title_zh: 精英少年选手正变得惊人地快。欢迎来到“跑道通胀”时代
rubric_en: Four-minute miles are almost routine. Middle schoolers are beating college runners. Inside the youth track boom that’s turning kids and teens into speed machines.
rubric_zh: 四分钟英里几乎成了常态。中学生正在超越大学生选手。就在这场让孩子和青少年变成“速度机器”的青少年田径热潮背后。

paragraphs.en: A couple of years ago, my teenage son decided to spend the long New York winter running indoor track. An accomplished soccer player, he joined the team with the modest goal of staying fit during his main sport’s offseason, but before long his competitive streak kicked in. He trained like a demon for two months straight, brutalizing his legs and lungs with high-intensity workouts. The suffering paid off: His time in the 1,600-meter run (just shy of a mile) dropped so much that he earned a spot in our sectional championship, competing against runners from three nearby counties.
paragraphs.zh: 几年前，我的十几岁儿子决定在漫长的纽约冬天里去室内跑道训练。作为一名踢球技术不错的足球运动员，他加入球队的初衷不过是想在主项休赛期保持体能，但很快他的竞争欲就被激发出来了。他连续两个多月疯狂训练，用高强度课程狠狠折磨着双腿和肺部。这些苦功没有白费：他在1600米跑（略短于一英里）成绩大幅下降，以此拿到了进入分区锦标赛的名额，将要和来自三个相邻县的选手同场竞技。

paragraphs.en: My son had the race of his life at sectionals, shaving several seconds off his personal best. He also got absolutely smoked. The top three boys all finished in well under 4 minutes and 30 seconds. The next six broke 4:38. By the time my son collapsed across the finish line in second-to-last place, the winner was deep into his post-race stretching routine.
paragraphs.zh: 我儿子在分区赛中跑出了人生最好成绩，个人纪录又砍下了好几秒。但他也被彻底压制了。前三名男生都跑进了4分30秒以内。接下来六个人也都突破了4分38秒。等到我儿子第二名之后才到达终点线，身体几乎崩溃地倒下时，冠军已经进入了赛后的拉伸环节。

paragraphs.en: Had teenage runners at mid-tier meets always been that absurdly fast? Based on my limited knowledge of track, I suspected not, so I looked up the 1,600-meter results from the 2015 sectionals. Back then, only three boys broke 4:40, and my son would have finished in the middle of the pack. I was now curious whether kids beyond my provincial corner of New York were getting a lot faster too.
paragraphs.zh: 高中阶段的赛场上，参赛的少年跑者一直以来都这么离谱地快吗？基于我对田径有限的了解，我怀疑未必如此，于是我查了一下2015年分区赛的1600米成绩。当时只有三名男生跑进了4分40秒以内，而我儿子当时会排在中游。现在我又好奇，除了我在纽约这个偏僻角落之外，别的地区的孩子是否也在加速变快。

paragraphs.en: I followed a few track social media accounts, and soon the algorithms deluged me with highlights of young runners obliterating records on what seemed like a daily basis. A 12th-grade girl from Ohio ran an indoor 2-miler in an otherworldly 9:37.15, breaking a national high school record that had stood since 2013. A 10th-grade boy from Texas ran an outdoor 100-meter dash in 9.92 seconds, just a tenth of a second off the collegiate record. Crunch the data on any race in virtually any school-age meet and you’ll find that the top and median times have plunged since the late 2010s, often by several seconds.
paragraphs.zh: 我关注了几个田径类社交媒体账号，不久算法就几乎每天都向我推送年轻跑者打破记录的精彩片段。一个来自俄亥俄州的12年级女生在室内跑出2英里9分37.15秒，打破了一项自2013年以来一直存在的全国高中纪录。一个来自德克萨斯州的10年级男生在室外百米跑中跑出9.92秒，仅差大学纪录0.1秒。把目光放到几乎任何一场校园年龄组比赛上，你都会发现，冠军和中位成绩自2010年代后期以来都明显下滑，常常快了好几秒。

paragraphs.en: My favorite videos were made by a former University of Pittsburgh hurdler named Shyheim Wright, a master at channeling the bewilderment of track fans. “Pack it up, pack it up right now!” he marveled over footage of a ninth-grade girl from Georgia running 100 meters in 11.01 seconds, a time that made her faster than all but the very best college sprinters. “I’m so glad I don’t run no more, I’m so glad. The times now is getting ridiculous.”
paragraphs.zh: 我最喜欢的视频是前匹兹堡大学跨栏选手Shyheim Wright拍的，他非常擅长捕捉赛道观众的困惑情绪。“赶紧撤吧，马上撤吧！”他看着一位来自佐治亚州的九年级女孩跑100米的画面感叹，她用11.01秒完成这项比赛，这个成绩让她快得只比最顶尖的大学短跑选手还慢。 “我真高兴自己不再跑步了，真的很高兴。现在的成绩真是快到离谱了。”

paragraphs.en: The track world, I discovered, has coined a name for this much-discussed phenomenon of kids getting ultra-fast: trackflation. In June 2025 at the New Balance Nationals, an annual championship that draws around 6,000 top youth athletes, more than 100 kids beat previous meet records. “There might now be a hundred kids that run under 4:10 in the full mile,” says Will Rodgers, who ran track at the University of Alabama in Huntsville in the early 2010s and now co-owns RunningLane, a coaching and events business. “Back when we were competing in high school, it might’ve been like 20 at max.”
paragraphs.zh: 我发现，赛道圈内已为如今大家热议的孩子们超高速成长这一现象起了个名字：trackflation（“赛道通胀”）。2025年6月在纽百伦全国少年锦标赛上——这是一项每年举办、吸引约6,000名顶尖青少年运动员的比赛——有超过100名孩子打破了以往的赛事纪录。 “现在可能已经有一百多名孩子能在一英里跑进4分10秒，”阿拉巴马大学亨茨维尔分校（University of Alabama in Huntsville）短跑老将兼现任RunningLane联合创始人的Will Rodgers说。RunningLane是一家提供指导与赛事服务的公司。“在我们高中比赛的时候，顶多也就20个吧。”

paragraphs.en: I had to know why kids today are essentially breaking track. And that meant trying to understand the dramatic shift happening at the sport’s roots.
paragraphs.zh: 我必须弄清楚，为什么当代孩子们几乎在打破一切赛道常识。为此，我需要去理解这项运动根基层面发生的巨大变化。

paragraphs.en: In early May, I drove down to Baltimore to attend the East Coast International Showcase, a two-day outdoor track meet sponsored by the shoe company Puma. One of its main draws was an 800-meter race featuring two fixtures of the elite youth circuit: Henry Bartle of Carmel, California, and Gunner Hammett of Williamsburg, Virginia.
paragraphs.zh: 今年5月初，我开车去了巴尔的摩，参加了由球鞋公司彪马（Puma）赞助的为期两天的户外赛道比赛——东海岸国际邀请赛（East Coast International Showcase）。这项比赛的一大亮点是800米决赛，参赛者包括青少年精英赛场上的两位常客：来自加州加梅尔（Carmel）的Henry Bartle和来自弗吉尼亚州威廉斯堡（Williamsburg）的Gunner Hammett。

paragraphs.en: The youngs are facing problems that didn't even exist half a generation ago. They’re handling it better than you think.
paragraphs.zh: 这些年轻人正在面对的问题，是半代人前几乎不存在的。他们应对这些挑战，比你想象的要更从容。

paragraphs.en: As these two stars warmed up alongside Morgan State University’s faded blue track, I spotted Hammett’s dad wearing a custom hoodie that listed his son’s credentials: “2 Time World Record Holder 800m, 18 Time All-American, 5-Time National Record Holder.”
paragraphs.zh: 当这两位小将与摩根州立大学那条褪色的蓝色跑道并肩热身时，我看到哈米特的父亲穿着一件定制连帽衫，上面写着他儿子的履历：“800米两次世界纪录保持者、18次全美精英入选（All-American）奖、5次全国纪录保持者。”

paragraphs.en: Those achievements were all the more impressive given Hammett’s tender age: He was 13, just a bit younger than the 14-year-old Bartle. Both boys were still weeks away from their eighth-grade graduations.
paragraphs.zh: 考虑到哈米特还这么小，这些成绩更显得令人印象深刻：他才13岁，只比14岁的巴特尔小一点。两名男孩离八年级毕业都还差几个星期。

paragraphs.en: The thin-limbed, bleached-blond Bartle ran the two-lap race in a personal best of 2:03.52, an outrageously good time. Three years ago that would have been enough to win the middle-school 800 meters at the New Balance Nationals. But he was nowhere close to Hammett, who coasted to a 1:57.75—a performance that put him on par with his home state’s fastest high school runners.
paragraphs.zh: 身材瘦削、金发发白的巴特尔在两圈比赛中跑出2:03.52的个人最好成绩，这一成绩惊人地好。三年前，这样的成绩足以拿下新平衡全国邀请赛的中学800米冠军。但在他同场比赛中的成绩还是远远不及哈米特，后者轻松跑进1:57.75——这一表现使他已与本州最快的高中跑者并驾齐驱。

paragraphs.en: “Guess I got smoked by you again,” a genial Bartle told Hammett as they gasped for air past the finish line. Bartle has, unfortunately, grown accustomed to falling short. Seven weeks earlier, he and another kid both shattered California’s age-group record for the indoor mile, but Bartle still finished six seconds off the podium. In the one-line caption for his Instagram post about that race, Bartle captured the essence of trackflation: “Living in a time where a state record still doesn’t medal at Nationals.”
paragraphs.zh: “看来又被你吊打了。”温和的巴特尔在越过终点线、气喘吁吁时对哈米特说。巴特尔不幸已经习惯了失手。七周前，他和另一位男孩一起打破了加州室内一英里年龄组纪录，但巴特尔仍落后领奖台六秒。就在他发关于那场比赛的 Instagram 帖子里，他用一句单行标题写下了“Trackflation”的核心：“在一个州纪录仍然拿不到全国赛奖牌的时代中生活。”

paragraphs.en: Once Bartle had recouped enough oxygen to function, he gave me a thoughtful breakdown of his recent progress. “Last year, even if I had somewhat of an injury, I was very stubborn and I trained through it,” he told me. Now he’s prioritizing recovery as part of his regimen, and he believes a sub-two-minute 800 and a sub-4:30 mile are well within his grasp.
paragraphs.zh: 巴特尔刚补足足够的氧气可以正常说话时，就向我仔细梳理了他最近的训练进展。“去年，尽管我当时有点受伤，我还是很倔强地硬撑着训练。”他对我说。现在他把恢复训练作为训练计划的一部分来重视，并且他认为自己完全有可能完成低于 2 分钟的 800 米和低于 4 分 30 秒的一英里。

paragraphs.en: Until seven or eight years ago, few eighth-grade runners operated with Bartle’s degree of maturity and determination. There were no middle-school divisions at the big national meets, and 13- and 14-year-olds rarely put serious weekly mileage on their legs. But perhaps inevitably, the youth sports boom expanded to include track.
paragraphs.zh: 直到七八年前，几乎没有多少八年级跑者能达到巴特尔这种成熟度和决心。全国大型比赛几乎没有中学组，13 岁和 14 岁的孩子也很少会给腿脚安排“认真”的高周跑量。可这场青少年体育热潮似乎也势必把跑道行业也卷了进去。

paragraphs.en: As millions of exhausted parents can attest, sports such as soccer and baseball have become dauntingly competitive over the past generation. Even for third and fourth graders, there’s a constant scramble for spots on prestigious travel teams, followed by nerve-racking fights for playing time. Some families decided they could avoid that madness by steering their kids toward track, which had relatively few participants at its entry-level ranks.
paragraphs.zh: 正如无数精疲力尽的家长所能证明的，过去一代里，像足球和棒球这样的运动已变得异常激烈。哪怕是三、四年级孩子，也要为了在有名望的旅行队中争夺名额而不断内卷，之后又要为每分钟上场时间和位置进行紧张厮杀。一些家庭认为可以通过让孩子转向田径来避开这种疯狂，因为田径的入门层级参赛人数长期以来都相对少。

paragraphs.en: Grade schoolers began flocking to local track clubs. Dennis Robinson, head coach of the Zephyrs Track Club in Chicago, told me that for most of the 2010s he only managed to sign up high schoolers. “We were concerned about the future of our club,” he said. “Now we’re starting to see more and more younger athletes. And when I say young, I mean we’ve had to turn away athletes who are 4 years old.” Another club I spoke with, the Youth Track Foundation in Portland, Oregon, signed up 60 kids in its first two years of existence and has a lengthy waiting list.
paragraphs.zh: 年级较小的孩子开始大量加入本地跑道俱乐部。芝加哥 Zephyrs Track Club 的主教练丹尼斯·罗宾逊告诉我，在2010年代的大部分时间里，他只能招到高中生。“我们当时很担心俱乐部的未来。”他说，“现在我们开始看到越来越多更年轻的运动员。如果我说‘年轻’，我的意思是我们已经不得不拒绝一些四岁的孩子。至于我采访的另一个俱乐部——俄勒冈州波特兰的 Youth Track Foundation，在成立头两年就招募了60名孩子，并且已经有了一份很长的候补名单。”

paragraphs.en: These clubs have also benefited from track’s growing reputation as an antidote to the politics and favoritism that plague less objective sports. “I have so many parents that join our club, and they’re just like, ‘This is so great, because we had this terrible experience with the baseball team because this parent was coaching and he never let my kid pitch,’” says Brad Peterson, a cofounder of HP Distance, a track club in Indiana. “But in track there’s no argument.” The stopwatch determines everything.
paragraphs.zh: 这些俱乐部也受益于田径日益被视为一种对抗缺乏客观性的运动中的政治与偏袒现象的解药。“我们接待了很多家长加入俱乐部，他们会说：‘太好了，因为我在棒球队经历过糟糕的情况，那时教练是家长，而且他总是不让我的孩子上场投球。’”这是印第安纳一所田径俱乐部 HP Distance 的共同创始人布拉德·彼得森说的。“但在田径里没有争论的空间。”秒表决定一切。

paragraphs.en: The rise of track clubs was apparent on the second day of the East Coast International Showcase, when I watched wave after wave of kids aged 5 to 9 compete in the 100 meters. The many standouts ran times that would be dazzling for boys or girls several years older, and I took note of their efficient mechanics—evidence, it seemed to me, that they’re already being coached to develop good habits.
paragraphs.zh: 在东海岸国际展示赛的第二天，田径俱乐部崛起的迹象就一目了然。我看着5到9岁的孩子一波接一波参加100米比赛。许多突出者的成绩都足以让年纪大几岁的男孩或女孩惊叹不已。我还注意到他们动作的高效性——这似乎证明，他们已经在被教练早早地培养出良好的动作习惯。

paragraphs.en: When talented elementary-age kids from the club system move into their tweens and early teens, they’re primed to start the grueling training that used to be reserved for high schoolers. “I’m asking middle-school girls to do eight 400s in 75 seconds, so at a 5-minute-mile pace,” says Peterson. “Whereas it used to be the middle-school program was, ‘Hey, let’s jog 20 minutes and let’s go play Capture the Flag,’ right?”
paragraphs.zh: 当这些俱乐部体系里有天赋的小学生逐步进入青春前期和少年早期时，他们已经为过去只属于高中生的高强度训练做好了准备。彼得森说：“我现在让初中女生做八次400米冲刺，目标75秒，也就是以每英里5分钟的配速。” “而以前的初中训练通常是这样的：‘好吧，我们慢跑20分钟，然后去玩捉人游戏吧？’”

paragraphs.en: Kids are increasingly doing that training in so-called “super shoes” such as the Nike Vaporfly 4 or New Balance FuelCell SuperComp Elite v5, both of which now often sell for well under $200. These shoes’ carbon plates and ultralight foams help return kinetic energy to the wearer’s body with every step. In races, super shoes have been proven to boost performance by 2 to 3 percent, which translates to roughly a second per 400-meter lap.
paragraphs.zh: 越来越多的孩子在所谓“超级鞋”中进行这类训练，例如 Nike Vaporfly 4 和 New Balance FuelCell SuperComp Elite v5，这两款鞋目前通常都能低于 200 美元买到。这些鞋子里的碳板和超轻泡沫可以在每一步里把动能更有效地返回给穿着者的身体。在比赛中，超级鞋已被证明能提升 2% 到 3% 的成绩，约等于每 400 米一圈快约一秒。

paragraphs.en: Geoff Burns, a sports physiologist with the US Olympic & Paralympic Committee, believes the shoes’ energy-return component decreases the traumatic stress endured by young bones and muscles. “If you’re able to do that same work but not have that mechanical collateral damage or nearly as much of it, then you’re just not building up as much scar tissue that could potentially inhibit performance,” he says. Instead of diverting lots of cellular energy to the healing process after every workout, the body can dedicate more resources to building strength.
paragraphs.zh: 美国奥林匹克与残奥会委员会的体育生理学家 Geoff Burns 认为，这些鞋子的能量回弹部分降低了年轻骨骼和肌肉承受的创伤性压力。“如果你能完成同样的训练，但没有那么多机械性附带损伤，或者几乎没有，那么你就不会形成过多可能抑制表现的瘢痕组织，”他表示。“与其在每次训练后把大量细胞能量都耗在修复过程上，身体就能把更多资源用于增强力量。”

paragraphs.en: As middle schoolers have gotten faster, big-name meets have made room for them. New Balance Nationals added its middle-school division in 2023, and other prominent track events followed suit. Athletes like Bartle now spend a significant chunk of every winter and spring traveling across the country.
paragraphs.zh: 随着初中生变得更快，知名赛事也为他们让出了空间。New Balance Nationals 于 2023 年新增了初中组，其他有影响力的田径赛事随后也纷纷效仿。像 Bartle 这样的运动员，现在每个冬季和春季都会花去相当大的一段时间在全国范围内奔波参赛。

paragraphs.en: The results make track old-timers shake their heads with wonder. In April, for example, an eighth-grade boy shattered the national middle-school record for the mile (he ran it sub-4:18). Just two weeks later, an eighth-grade girl ran the 400 meters faster than any US high schooler had at that point in the year (she clocked 51.58).
paragraphs.zh: 这些结果让许多田径老将听了都要惊讶地摇头。比如说，在 4 月，一名八年级男生打破了全国初中一英里纪录（他跑进了 4:18 以内）。仅仅两周后，一名八年级女生跑出了 400 米快于当年美国高中生在当时所有人的成绩（她的成绩是 51.58 秒）。

paragraphs.en: It’s not just the superstars who are getting faster. The runners in the pack behind them are picking up the pace, too, and they’re transforming what’s considered a decent time. “I think I have 10 middle-school girls who run between a 4:56 and a 5:16 mile,” says Peterson. “Ten years ago, you’d be like, ‘That’s insane.’ Now it’s nothing.” When these kids head to high school, they have the strength and experience to flourish right away. But given the sky-high expectations they face, the intensity can come at a cost.
paragraphs.zh: 这不仅仅是超级明星变得更快了，随队的其他跑者也在提速，他们正在重塑人们对“合格成绩”的定义。“我认识大约10个初中女生，跑一英里在4分56秒到5分16秒之间，”皮特森说，“十年前你会觉得‘太疯狂了’。现在这简直是家常便饭。”这些孩子一进高中，就已经具备了立刻崭露头角所需的力量和经验。但在他们面临的天花板般高期待下，这种强度也可能要付出代价。

paragraphs.en: There has long been a vast discrepancy between the number of high schoolers who run track and the money their programs receive. Outdoor track and field boasts more high school athletes than any other sport, with over 1.1 million participants—a figure likely inflated by the fact that teams tend to make few if any cuts. (The second-most populous co-ed sport, basketball, has fewer than 900,000 players.) Many athletic departments treat track like an afterthought, undeserving of the resources needed to hire coaches with real expertise.
paragraphs.zh: 长期以来，参加田径运动的高中生人数与其项目经费之间一直存在巨大差距。户外田径队员数量超过110万，超越了所有其他运动，是规模最大的高中运动项目之一，这一数字可能因球队通常几乎不设淘汰而被放大。（第二大男女混合项目篮球的参赛人数不足90万。）许多体育部门把田径当成附属项目，不愿投入足够资源去聘请真正专业的教练。

paragraphs.en: “When I was coming through, my coach was a retired Marine who had never run a step in his life,” says Caryn Gehrke, an all–Ivy League runner at Columbia University in the early 2000s who now coaches in Ann Arbor, Michigan. “His entire philosophy was the more you hurt in practice, the more you can hurt in a race,” which is possibly the most counterproductive advice a runner can hear.
paragraphs.zh: “我当初在跑步时，教练是位退休海军陆战队员，他一生中几乎没真正跑过步，”科林斯大学2000年代初的一位全程常春藤联盟跑者凯琳·格克（Caryn Gehrke）说，她如今在密歇根州安娜堡执教。“他一贯的理念就是：训练时受得越痛，比赛时就能承受越多痛苦。”这可能是跑者听到过最具反效果的建议。

paragraphs.en: Today, a high school runner can scrutinize workout videos posted by pros or simply ask ChatGPT “How do I run a sub-2:00 800?” and develop a more effective training plan than anything their forebears were using in 2016. But for runners with any financial means, hiring a private coach for one-on-one guidance has become commonplace. It’s easy to find former collegiate runners on Instagram who are eager to capitalize on their years of geeking out on training methods. These coaches have popularized techniques such as heat acclimatization, in which athletes overexert themselves in sweltering conditions—say, by running on a treadmill next to a space heater—so that their bodies will produce extra plasma. (This is sometimes referred to as “legal blood doping.”)
paragraphs.zh: 如今，高中跑者可以仔细研究职业运动员发布的训练视频，也可以直接问ChatGPT“我怎么才能跑进2分00秒以内的800米？”，并据此制定比2016年前辈使用的任何方案都更有效的训练计划。对于有经济条件的跑者来说，雇请私人教练进行一对一指导已成了普遍做法。你很容易在Instagram上找到前大学跑者，他们热衷于将自己多年的训练方法研究经验变现。这些教练推广了如热适应训练等技术，让运动员在闷热环境里过度消耗体能——例如在跑步机旁开着暖风机——从而让身体产生更多血浆。（这有时也被称作“合法的输血增强”。）

paragraphs.en: They’re also a big reason why high school kids are dabbling in double-threshold training, which was pioneered by Norwegian pros. This approach involves doing a hard morning workout, then analyzing a drop of the athlete’s blood to see how much lactic acid their muscles produced. The runner then adjusts their pace up or down during a killer second workout of the day.
paragraphs.zh: 这也是高中生开始尝试双阈值训练的主要原因之一。这一方法由挪威职业跑者率先提出，做法是先进行一场高强度的晨间训练，然后分析一滴运动员血样，看肌肉产生了多少乳酸。随后，跑者会在当天最严酷的第二次训练中，根据结果把配速往上或往下调整。

paragraphs.en: Private coaches also preach about proper nutrition and supplements. Survey the trash cans at any meet and you’ll see empty tubs of Maurten Bicarb System, a sodium bicarbonate powder that runners consume en masse before races. The substance suppresses the development of lactic acid in muscles, allowing runners to stave off fatigue for a tad longer than normal. According to a 2021 meta survey, bicarb may boost performance by 1.5 percent, or around three seconds per mile.
paragraphs.zh: 私人教练也在大力宣传科学饮食和补剂。走到任何一场比赛的垃圾桶旁，你都会看到装过 Maurten Bicarb System 的空桶，那是跑步者赛前大量服用的碳酸氢钠粉。该物质可抑制肌肉中乳酸的产生，让跑者比平时更久地延缓疲劳。根据2021年的一项汇总研究，重碳酸氢钠可能带来约1.5%的成绩提升，也就是每英里大约快3秒。

paragraphs.en: There’s plenty of chatter that some teen athletes are emulating the pros by ingesting banned substances—a cheat that’s almost impossible to detect, since there’s virtually no drug testing at the high school level. “I’m not saying I’m 100 percent on this, but I can pick out the athletes who are on it,” says Timothy Goldsack, a cofounder of the coaching service RunCCG, who cites human growth hormone and testosterone-replacement therapy as two popular performance enhancers. He blames familial pressure on kids to use track as a ticket to college: “Parents are crazy, you know that.”
paragraphs.zh: 有不少声音称，一些青少年运动员在模仉职业选手，服用禁用物质来“加速”，这几乎无法被发现，因为高中阶段几乎没有药检。RunCCG训练服务的联合创始人 Timothy Goldsack 说：“我并不是说自己100%确定，但我能认出来哪些运动员在用。”他说到的人体生长激素和睾酮替代疗法是两种常见的兴奋提升手段。他把“让孩子把田径当作升学捷径”的家庭压力归咎于此：“你知道的，家长们很疯狂。”

paragraphs.en: To impress college scouts, high schoolers must travel to the national meets that are known for producing the most trackflationary times. These meets tend to have bouncy polyurethane tracks, favorable weather, or pace lights that keep runners apprised of the tempos needed to break records. A few well-known teen runners receive airfare and hotel rooms for gracing these meets with their presence, but most competitors spend small fortunes to attend. “If a family has the money to invest in that and it costs them $2,000 to fly a kid across the country to take 15 seconds off their time, and that all of a sudden opens the door to another tier of scholarship or whatever, that’s a pretty good investment,” says Geoff Burns of the US Olympic & Paralympic Committee.
paragraphs.zh: 为了吸引大学球探，许多高中生要前往全国性赛事，这些比赛以产出最“Trackflation”式成绩而闻名。这样的比赛常有有弹性的聚氨酯跑道、较有利的天气条件，或有配速灯帮助跑者随时掌握破纪录所需的节奏。少数知名青少年选手会因登场受邀而获得机票和酒店，但大多数参赛者都要花不少钱才能出席。“如果一个家庭愿意投入，并且为了让孩子在全国比赛中快15秒而花掉2,000美元，而这一下子就把他们推进了另一个级别的奖学金机会，那么这投资其实很划算，”美国奥运会与残奥会委员会（US Olympic & Paralympic Committee）的 Geoff Burns 说，“这算是一种很值得的投资。”

paragraphs.en: In mid-April, I spoke to Chris Catton, the other cofounder of RunCCG, as he prepared to coach several of his clients through a notoriously fast meet, the Asics Carolina Distance Carnival in North Carolina. “I have people who have flown here from Montana, North Dakota, Oklahoma, Rhode Island, New Jersey,” he told me by phone from his Airbnb in Charlotte. He was excited because the racing would take place at night, when times are fastest, and runners would be grouped together in heats according to their personal bests—a reliable way to get kids to push each other in a tight pack.
paragraphs.zh: 4月中旬，我在他位于夏洛特的 Airbnb 打电话时采访了 RunCCG 的另一位联合创始人 Chris Catton。那时他正准备指导几位客户参加一次臭名昭著、成绩极快的比赛——北卡罗来纳州的 Asics Carolina Distance Carnival。“我这儿有些运动员从蒙大拿州、北达科他州、俄克拉荷马州、罗德岛州、新泽西州飞来。”他告诉我。对他来说，这次比赛很激动人心，因为比赛安排在晚上进行，那时成绩通常最快，而且跑者会按个人最佳成绩分组跑同一热身组，这是一种非常有效的办法，能让孩子们在紧密小组中互相逼迫、相互竞争。

paragraphs.en: Two days later, Catton texted me a heads-up to check out the Carnival’s results. They were staggering: In the high school boys’ mile, the top 14 finishers all went sub-4:10. Five middle-school girls had broken the 5-minute barrier. Catton said that one of his clients, a ninth-grade girl who ran the 3,200 meters, had set an Oklahoma state record at the Carnival, yet only managed to come in 14th—half a minute behind the winner.
paragraphs.zh: 两天后，Catton 给我发消息提醒去看 Carnival 的成绩，结果令人震惊：在高中男生英里跑里，前14名全部跑进了4分10秒以内。五名中学生女生也突破了5分钟大关。Catton 说，他的一个客户——一名九年级女生，在3200米项目上跑出了俄克拉荷马州的州纪录，尽管如此，在这场比赛中她仍排在第14位，落后冠军半分钟。

paragraphs.en: The social media content generated by these meets has turned a number of high schoolers into influencers. Some of them get sponsorship deals. Most receive perks rather than money—notably loads of free swag that they’re encouraged to flaunt online—though that may change as brands increasingly realize how much high school stars can shape the tastes of young consumers.
paragraphs.zh: 这些比赛产生的社交媒体内容把不少高中生推成了“网红”。其中一些人甚至拿到了赞助合作。大多数人得到的并不是现金，而是各种“好处”——尤其是大量免费的周边，他们被鼓励在网上炫耀；不过随着品牌越来越清楚高中的明星对年轻消费者口味的影响力，这一情况可能会改变。

paragraphs.en: I watched one of those sought-after celebrities, 17-year-old Natalie Dumas, compete at the East Coast International Showcase. The New Jersey native had been one of the most recognizable faces of trackflation for nearly a year, ever since she’d won an incredible three races at the New Balance Nationals in June 2025. In Baltimore, she warmed up for a mere 20 minutes before demolishing the field in the 400-meter hurdles. She looked almost bored as she finished in 58.78; it wasn’t until I checked her Instagram later that I realized it was (at least at that moment) the fastest a high school girl had run the race this year.
paragraphs.zh: 我观看了这群被追捧的“红人”中的一位，17岁的 Natalie Dumas，在 East Coast International Showcase 参赛。自从她在2025年6月的 New Balance Nationals 闪电般赢下三场比赛以来，这位新泽西州的女孩已近一年成为“Trackflation”最醒目的面孔之一。在巴尔的摩，她只热身了短短20分钟，就在400米栏中彻底碾压全场。她完成比赛时看起来几乎有些无聊，我后来在她的 Instagram 上才发现：至少在当时，她就是今年高中女生跑这项最快三快的。

paragraphs.en: When I caught up with Dumas right after the race, she implied that she would have run much faster if she wasn’t in such high demand. “I ran Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, and today’s Saturday, so this is my fourth meet this week,” she told me. “This is my sixth event in the past four days.”
paragraphs.zh: 我在比赛后立刻追上Dumas时，她暗示如果没有这么紧张安排的话，她本可以跑得更快。“我周三、周四、周五都跑了，今天又是星期六，这是我本周的第四场比赛，”她对我说。“这是我过去四天里的第六个项目。”

paragraphs.en: For a brief moment in April, a stunning video was everywhere in my Instagram feed. It showed a 12-year-old girl from Utah running the mile in 4:40.98, a time that crushed the previous world record for her age group by nearly six seconds. She might have run even faster had she not elected to celebrate with the Scuba Dance a few steps short of the finish line.
paragraphs.zh: 四月有那么一小段时间，一段令人惊叹的视频在我的Instagram动态里铺天盖地。画面里是一位来自犹他州的12岁女孩，跑出4分40.98秒的英里成绩，几乎打破了她同龄组的世界纪录，提前近6秒。她之所以没跑得更快，可能是因为在离终点线还有几步时，她选择跳出，去跳一段“Scuba Dance”庆祝动作。

paragraphs.en: The feat made Shyheim Wright, the content creator who specializes in highlighting the most ludicrous examples of trackflation, lose his mind. “Nah, bro, nah, bro, no way!” he began his Instagram post. “Bro, nobody was even in the camera with her, bro. There was other people in the race. I didn’t see nobody.” (“She was in my heat,” wrote one commenter, a seventh grader from North Carolina who ran her personal best in the race. “I didn’t see her once, she was so far ahead.”)
paragraphs.zh: 这条事迹让专门挖掘最离谱“Trackflation”案例的内容创作者Shyheim Wright完全疯了。“哥们，别闹，真没有吧！”他在Instagram贴文里开场道。“哥们，赛道上甚至没人在镜头里拍到她，哥们。比赛里有其他人。我一个都没看到她。”（“我是在她同组跑的。”一位北卡罗来纳州的七年级女生留言，她在那场比赛中跑出了个人最好成绩。“我根本没见到她一次，她远远跑在前面。”）

paragraphs.en: To many longtime track observers, however, the video was a cause for alarm. Multiple coaches and researchers told me this particular world record crystallized their fears for the safety of young runners who feel compelled to keep topping their peers. “When you see these amazing performances, it’s also just the tip of the spear of a lot of people also falling apart,” says Douglas Casa, a kinesiology professor at the University of Connecticut who studies elite athletes. “I’m not excited about all this progress. It just means that they’re working so much harder at a younger age.” He’s especially worried about young girls who specialize in middle- to long-distance events, since running the mileage required to excel in these races can delay puberty, leading to long-term issues such as lower bone density.
paragraphs.zh: 然而对许多资深田径观察者来说，这段视频却是个警讯。多位教练和研究者告诉我，这一世界纪录巩固了他们对年轻跑者安全的担忧：这些孩子觉得必须一直超越同龄人。“当你看到这些惊人的表现时，这也只是许多人身心也在失衡的一种冰山一角，”康涅狄克大学运动生理学教授Douglas Casa说，他长期研究精英运动员。“我对这种进步并不兴奋。这只说明他们在更小的年龄就承受了更狠的训练。”他尤其担心专攻中长距离项目的年轻女孩，因为要在这类项目中取得优秀成绩，需要跑的里程会延迟她们的青春发育，进而引发长期问题，例如骨密度偏低。

paragraphs.en: Burnout has always been a major problem in track, a sport anchored in athletes’ ability to sop up unnatural amounts of punishment. “I’ve spent my whole life running a race fueled by fear, never-ending expectations, and external validation,” wrote Stanford University 800-meter specialist Roisin Willis in a 2025 Instagram post about overcoming her mental health struggles; around the same time, Chloe Foerster, an All American middle-distance runner at the University of Washington, went public about her serious anxiety, which often caused her to vomit before races.
paragraphs.zh: “燃尽”一直是田径运动的重大问题，这项运动依赖运动员承受非自然程度的高强度惩罚。斯坦福大学800米选手罗辛·威利斯（Roisin Willis）在2025年的一篇Instagram帖子中写道：“我这辈子都在恐惧、无休止的期待和外界认可推动下去跑赛道”；与此同时，华盛顿大学的全美级中长跑选手克洛伊·弗斯特（Chloe Foerster）也公开了自己严重焦虑的问题，这种焦虑常常让她在比赛前呕吐。

paragraphs.en: One 2022 study looked at the careers of 67,600 top-ranked teenage track-and-field athletes. Less than a quarter of 16- and 17-year-olds achieved comparable success past those ages, a transition rate the study authors described as “low.” Burns told me the results are even worse for kids who dominate at 16 or younger. “It’s a Faustian bargain,” he says. “Like, do you hyper-specialize and develop as a middle schooler and high schooler so you can get access and get scholarships and open all these doors, but it comes at the cost of actually being able to be good at it later?”
paragraphs.zh: 一项2022年的研究分析了67,600名顶尖青少年田径运动员的职业轨迹。16到17岁的运动员中，少于四分之一的人能在过了这个年龄后取得可比的成功，研究作者把这一转化率形容为“偏低”。Burns告诉我，对16岁及以下就占据领先位置的孩子来说，这个结果甚至更糟。“这是一次与魔鬼做交易，”他说。“也就是说，你在初中和高中时期过度专科化、快速发展，借此获得机会、赢得奖学金、打开许多大门，但代价是以后不一定还能真正坚持做得优秀吗？”

paragraphs.en: I didn’t meet a single young runner who showed any trace of having pondered that question—naivete is one of the blessings of youth. They were instead wrapped up in envying the kids whose increasingly impressive victories get pumped into their phones every day: “When you see a girl run a faster time, all of them are trying to attack it, and they all have the mindset that they want to win,” said Dumas, the 17-year-old phenom.
paragraphs.zh: 我没有见过一个年轻跑者曾显露出思考过这个问题的痕迹——天真是青春的某种幸运。相反，他们都沉浸在羡慕那些成绩越来越惊人的同龄人之中，这些同伴的战绩每天都会被推送到他们的手机里。“当你看到一个女孩跑得更快时，所有人都会去试着突破她的成绩，而且他们都抱着想赢的心态，”17岁的天才运动员杜马斯（Dumas）说。

paragraphs.en: Whenever it seems like times are about to plateau in a certain race, another 14- or 15-year-old emerges to set a new standard and stoke the competition. (That Utah girl who set the internet alight with a 4:40 mile in April? She beat her own record by two seconds in June.) Jesse Williams, a meet organizer in Seattle, used to give coaches a rough estimate of what times would earn their athletes a spot at the Brooks PR, an elite invitational that he helps oversee. He had to stop—records are falling too fast for Williams to keep up.
paragraphs.zh: 每当某个项目的成绩似乎到了天花板，就会又有一位14岁或15岁的新秀冒出来，树立新的标杆并点燃竞争。（就是那个4月用4分40秒跑完一英里、在网络上引发轰动的犹他州女孩？她在6月又把自己纪录提前了2秒。）西雅图的一名比赛组织者杰西·威廉姆斯（Jesse Williams）过去会给教练们一个大致估算，告诉他们多少成绩才能让运动员拿到他协助组织的精英邀请赛Brooks PR的参赛名额。但他现在不得不放弃这个预估了——成绩下降得太快，他跟不上了。

paragraphs.en: All that progress, of course, is built atop a foundation of pain, and I had to ask the kids why they love a sport that primarily tests their ability to endure agony. The answer that stuck with me most came from Dane Szczepkowski, an 18-year-old sprinter from SPIRE Academy in Geneva, Ohio. I caught him during a rough moment in Baltimore, mere minutes after he’d finished 39th in the 400 meters; he was clearly struggling as he tried to stretch away the damage he’d done to his legs. When I asked him why he’d become so devoted to running, he thought for a beat before offering an emphatic answer. “It’s hard,” he said. “It’s hard.”
paragraphs.zh: 当然，所有这些进步都建立在痛苦的基础上，我不得不问孩子们，为什么他们热爱一项主要考验他们忍受痛楚能力的运动。最让我印象深刻的回答来自18岁的丹·谢切科夫斯基（Dane Szczepkowski），他是俄亥俄州日内瓦的 SPIRE Academy 的一名短跑运动员。我在巴尔的摩遇见他时，他刚刚完成了400米比赛，名次是第39名，后来他正在艰难地拉伸受损的双腿。当我问他为什么会如此热爱跑步时，他停顿了一下，给出了一个强调的回答： “很难，真的很难。”

paragraphs.en: I’d heard that sentiment from a lot of runners. They had chosen track specifically because it’s an unglamorous grind—a lonely contest between them and a pitiless clock. The joy is in learning to appreciate the cause-and-effect relationship between arduous labor and incremental progress, even when that progress delivers a middle-of-the-pack result. Sophia Belcher, a high school junior from the Dallas area who won a Texas state title in the 800 meters this May, told me how the never-ending demands of track have been her rock through the most difficult moments of adolescence: “In a world where I think a lot of things are changing and not constant, I kind of had running that I could focus on, that would always be there.”
paragraphs.zh: 我从很多跑步者那里都听到过这种感受。他们之所以选择田径，是因为这项运动本质上是一种不加修饰、重复枯燥的磨练——一场他们与无情秒表之间的孤独较量。快乐在于学会欣赏“艰苦劳动”与“点滴进步”之间的因果关系，即使那种进步只带来中等水平的名次。德州达拉斯地区的高中三年级学生索菲娅·贝尔彻（Sophia Belcher）在今年5月赢得了800米德州州冠军，她告诉我，田径无休止的要求在她青春期最艰难的时候，像一块定海神针： “在一个我觉得很多事情都在变化、不再稳定的世界里，我有一件可以专注的事——跑步，它总是在那里。”

paragraphs.en: There’s obviously nothing new about unmoored teens searching for structure, but track’s rise does feel connected to the unique tumult of the past few years. Kids are understandably reeling from the echoes of the pandemic, social media, AI. Track just might be the ideal sport for those who tend to turn inward when looking to build self-belief. Amid the acceleration of everything, there’s clarity to be found in trying to get a tenth of a second faster.
paragraphs.zh: 青少年缺乏支点、急需秩序并不是什么新鲜事，但田径热潮确实让人感觉与过去几年独特的动荡有关。孩子们自然会被疫情、社交媒体和人工智能留下的余波影响。对于那些在建立自我信心时倾向于向内收拢的人来说，田径也许正是理想运动。在一切都在加速变化的时代，在追求百毫秒更快一点的过程中，反而能找到一种清晰。

paragraphs.en: My son was good-natured about his thumping loss at the 2025 sectionals, but that race turned out to be the apex of his running career. He ran winter track again as a senior but his heart wasn’t in it—he grew averse to the harsh training for the 1,600 and dropped down to shorter races, where faster sprinters dominated. A second trip to sectionals wasn’t in the cards, which didn’t bother him; by season’s end, he’d become more interested in trying to learn how to kick field goals so he can walk on to his college’s football team in the fall.
paragraphs.zh: 我的儿子对自己在2025年分区赛上的惨败一度表现得很大度，但那场比赛最终成了他田径生涯的高峰。作为高三生他又参加了冬季田径，但他已经没有了热情——他不再愿意为1600米承受那种严格训练，于是改跑更短的项目，却被跑得更快的选手碾压。第二次去分区赛不在他的计划中，这并没有困扰他；到赛季结束时，他反而更感兴趣于学习如何踢球门球，于是准备在秋季试着加入他所在大学的美式足球队。

paragraphs.en: But our family’s journey through track seems far from over. My 13-year-old daughter joined her middle-school team this spring, and after some trial and error, she settled on the 800 as her primary race. She gutted out some decent times at a few meets, and her coach rewarded her with a spot in a post-season meet held in a nearby college town.
paragraphs.zh: 但我们一家在田径上的这段旅程似乎还远未结束。今年春天，我13岁的女儿加入了中学田径队，经过一番尝试，她把800米定为自己的主打项目。她在几场比赛中拼尽全力跑出了还算不错的成绩，教练于是给她安排了一个到附近大学城举行的赛季后比赛名额。

paragraphs.en: She was nervous for days beforehand, and I tried to assure her that just showing up and running would be a victory—no one expects her to be the next Natalie Dumas. On the afternoon of the meet, though, she called me to report that things had gone much better than expected. Not only had she conquered her butterflies, she’d also won her heat. She’d looked over her shoulder with about 50 meters to go and realized that no one was even close to overtaking her. “I pulverized those other girls,” she said with evident glee.
paragraphs.zh: 赛前她紧张了好几天，我一直安慰她，能到场参赛并完成比赛就已经是胜利——没人指望她会成为下一个 Natalie Dumas。可在比赛当天下午，她给我打来电话，说一切都远超预期。她不仅克服了紧张，更赢了自己的分组赛。比赛还剩大约50米时，她回头一看，发现没有任何人接近可以超越她。“我把那些女孩全都碾压了。”她明显兴奋地说。

paragraphs.en: She’s still light-years away from a shout-out in a Shyheim Wright video. But in the era of trackflation, all things seem possible.
paragraphs.zh: 她离在 Shyheim Wright 的视频里被提名还差得远着呢。但在Trackflation时代，几乎一切都似乎有可能。

paragraphs.en: The images for this story were photographed at the New Balance Nationals Outdoor on June 21, 2026, at Franklin Field in Philadelphia.
paragraphs.zh: 本文配图摄于2026年6月21日，在费城富兰克林体育场举行的 New Balance Nationals Outdoor。

paragraphs.en: Let us know what you think about this article. Submit a letter to the editor at [email protected].
paragraphs.zh:
请告诉我们您对这篇文章的看法。请向编辑部投稿：
